title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 21: Incident Response
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 21: Incident Response. Domain: Security Operations (4.0).

questions.question: What are the six phases of the NIST incident response lifecycle?
answers: Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-Incident Activity
Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
Plan, Detect, Contain, Fix, Review, Close
Assess, Classify, Escalate, Resolve, Document
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-61 defines the IR lifecycle: Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Post-Incident Activity (lessons learned).

questions.question: What is a CSIRT?
answers: A Computer Security Incident Response Team responsible for handling security incidents
A network monitoring tool
A certificate authority for incident response
A cloud security inspection tool
questions.explanation: A CSIRT is a team (internal or external) that coordinates and executes the response to cybersecurity incidents, from detection through recovery.

questions.question: What is the goal of the containment phase in incident response?
answers: To limit the spread and impact of the incident while preserving evidence
To completely remove the threat from all systems
To restore systems to normal operation
To identify who caused the incident
questions.explanation: Containment limits the scope and damage of an ongoing incident. It can involve network isolation, disabling accounts, or other measures — while preserving evidence for forensics.

questions.question: What happens during the eradication phase of incident response?
answers: Removing the root cause of the incident (malware, backdoors, compromised accounts)
Notifying stakeholders about the incident
Restoring data from backups
Documenting the incident timeline
questions.explanation: Eradication eliminates the threat — removing malware, closing attack vectors, patching vulnerabilities, and ensuring the attacker has no remaining foothold.

questions.question: What is chain of custody in digital forensics?
answers: Documentation tracking who handled evidence and when, ensuring its integrity
A legal agreement between law enforcement and organizations
The process of encrypting forensic evidence
A timeline of the attack progression
questions.explanation: Chain of custody documents the collection, transfer, and handling of evidence to prove it was not tampered with, making it admissible in legal proceedings.

questions.question: What are Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)?
answers: Evidence artifacts that suggest a system has been breached (malicious IPs, hashes, registry keys)
Security vulnerabilities that have been exploited
Alerts generated by an IDS
Compliance violations found during an audit
questions.explanation: IOCs are forensic data points (file hashes, IP addresses, domain names, registry changes) that indicate a system has been or is being attacked.

questions.question: What is a tabletop exercise in incident response?
answers: A discussion-based simulation where stakeholders walk through an incident scenario
A live technical exercise testing actual response systems
A vulnerability scanning exercise
A physical security drill
questions.explanation: A tabletop exercise is a discussion-based activity where team members walk through a hypothetical incident scenario to identify gaps and test processes without live systems.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a post-incident review (lessons learned)?
answers: To identify what happened, what worked well, and how to improve future response
To assign blame for the incident
To calculate the financial cost of the incident
To notify regulatory bodies
questions.explanation: The lessons learned phase analyzes the incident to document what happened, evaluate the effectiveness of the response, and implement improvements to prevent recurrence.

questions.question: When collecting digital evidence, what is the order of volatility principle?
answers: Collect the most volatile (easily lost) data first — RAM before disk
Collect disk evidence before memory
Collect logs before all other evidence
Collect network data last
questions.explanation: Order of volatility dictates collecting evidence from most to least volatile: CPU registers/cache → RAM → swap/virtual memory → network data → disk → remote logs → archived media.

questions.question: What is SOAR in the context of incident response?
answers: Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response — automates repetitive IR tasks
Security Operations and Audit Review
A SIEM replacement tool
A threat intelligence sharing platform
questions.explanation: SOAR platforms automate incident response workflows (playbooks), orchestrate security tools, and enable faster response by reducing manual tasks.

questions.question: What is an incident response playbook?
answers: A documented set of procedures for responding to specific types of incidents
A training manual for security analysts
A list of known threat actors
A compliance checklist
questions.explanation: An IR playbook provides step-by-step procedures for responding to specific incident types (ransomware, data breach, phishing), ensuring consistent and effective responses.

questions.question: What is the difference between a BCP and a DRP?
answers: BCP focuses on continuing business operations; DRP focuses on restoring IT systems
BCP is for data recovery; DRP is for people safety
They are identical plans
DRP is broader than BCP
questions.explanation: A Business Continuity Plan (BCP) focuses on maintaining critical business functions during a disruption. A Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) focuses specifically on restoring IT systems and data.

questions.question: When must organizations report a data breach under GDPR?
answers: Within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach
Within 30 days of discovery
Immediately upon detection
Within 1 year of the breach occurring
questions.explanation: GDPR requires organizations to notify the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach that poses a risk to individuals.

questions.question: Why is RAM (memory) forensics important in incident response?
answers: RAM contains running processes, decrypted data, and network connections not visible on disk
RAM stores all log files for analysis
RAM evidence is easier to collect than disk evidence
RAM forensics is only needed for hardware failures
questions.explanation: Memory forensics reveals volatile evidence like running malware processes, decrypted credentials, encryption keys, and network connections that would be lost if the system is powered off.

questions.question: What should be the first step when law enforcement involvement is anticipated?
answers: Preserve evidence without modification and consult legal counsel before acting
Immediately wipe all affected systems
Notify the media
Share all logs publicly
questions.explanation: When legal action is possible, evidence must be preserved in its original state and handled per legal requirements. Premature actions can compromise admissibility or obstruct investigations.
